metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal AC (or extend AC4's ADR requirement) that records the outcome of the `refs/reconciler/*` blob-ref feasibility check and explicitly states which architecture path (ref->blob or ref->tiny-commit) was implemented, so the choice is auditable and AC2 remains consistent with the deliverable.
findings.finding: The GitHub blob-ref feasibility check ('C1 AC0') is described in the Design section as a load-bearing prerequisite that forks into two architectures (ref->blob vs ref->tiny-commit), but it is not captured in the Acceptance Criteria list and has no formal acceptance gate. An executor must make an architectural choice mid-stream with no AC to satisfy or record.
findings.location: Design section: 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven … verified FIRST (C1 AC0)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'The ADR includes the result of the GitHub blob-ref feasibility probe (C1 AC0) and documents whether the ref->blob or ref->tiny-commit path was taken; AC2's blob schema applies to whichever path is chosen.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and add to `reconcile-bridge.yml` the explicit fetch of `refs/reconciler/*` from `origin` before the reconciler step and the push of `refs/reconciler/*` back to `origin` after acquire/renew/release; confirm the refspec format (`+refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*`) and where in the step sequence it belongs.
findings.finding: The CI reconcile-bridge workflow has no step that fetches or pushes `refs/reconciler/*` — the plan specifies these refs as the new lock store but never describes the required additions to `reconcile-bridge.yml` (a new fetch step before the reconciler runs, and a push step after). The current commit-back step only pushes `HEAD:tickets`. Without an explicit refspec to bring `refs/reconciler/lock` from `origin` before acquire and push it back after acquire/renew/release, the ref-CAS protocol silently degrades to local-only and provides no cross-clone coordination.
findings.location: Design section (refs/reconciler/* as authoritative on `origin`); reconcile-bridge.yml commit-back step (line 209-271)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a workflow step immediately before 'Run reconciler': `git fetch origin '+refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*' || true` (graceful on first run when refs don't exist yet). The ref-lock push should be handled by the Python layer after acquire/renew/release via `git push origin refs/reconciler/lock refs/reconciler/gate` — specify this in the `_ref_lock.py` design rather than leaving it to the commit-back step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contingency path with AC1: either add a conditional AC1a ('if blob infeasible, ref -> single-commit with the same JSON blob as tree entry') or rewrite AC1 to cover both blob and commit variants so the feasibility fallback has a passing criterion.
findings.finding: AC1 hard-asserts 'bare ref -> blob' unconditionally, but the Design section explicitly provides a fallback contingency ('ref -> tiny-commit instead of ref -> blob') if GitHub rejects blob-pointing refs. If the C1 AC0 feasibility check fails and the implementer follows the contingency, AC1 as written is violated — the two sections point in opposite directions.
findings.location: Design section ('Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven') vs AC1 ('bare ref -> blob, not in the working tree')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC1: '…acquire is create-only CAS, release/steal is observed-oid CAS (blob variant preferred; single-commit variant if GitHub rejects blob-pointing refs — confirmed by C1 AC0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the rollback path for the `.gitattributes` migration: specify that it is idempotent, describe how to re-add `.reconciler-*` to `merge=ours` if rolling back to `lock_backend = file`, and note that the migration must run atomically per-repo (not across the fleet) so partial failure leaves each individual repo in a consistent state.
findings.finding: The migration that updates 'already-initialized trackers' (removing `.reconciler-*` from `.gitattributes` on existing tickets branches) has no specified rollback or partial-failure recovery path.
findings.location: AC item 5 / `_commands/init.py:57`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make AC1 (and AC2) conditional on the C1 AC0 feasibility spike result, or split them into two variant ACs (blob path / tiny-commit fallback path), so the Acceptance Criteria remain valid regardless of the GitHub capability outcome.
findings.finding: The plan commits a full design (AC1–AC7) to `git update-ref` pointing at a blob object, but whether GitHub's receive-pack accepts pushes of `refs/reconciler/*` pointing at a blob (not a commit) via `GITHUB_TOKEN` is explicitly described as 'load-bearing and unproven' — meaning the ACs are written against a capability whose existence is not yet confirmed.
findings.location: Design section: 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven … verified FIRST (C1 AC0); the contingency if it does not is ref -> tiny-commit instead of ref -> blob'; AC1: 'bare ref -> blob'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all coordinating clones (local + CI bridge) must adopt the same `lock_backend` value simultaneously (e.g., treat the config key as a cluster-wide setting, documented in the ADR and the cutover runbook), and add a startup assertion/warning if a clone detects lock-refs present while running in `file` mode (or vice versa).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a `lock_backend = file | ref` switch for staged rollout, but does not specify that ALL coordinating clones (local + GitHub-Actions bridge) must switch to the same backend simultaneously. During rollout, if one clone uses `lock_backend = file` and another uses `lock_backend = ref`, the two backends are fully disjoint — each side ignores the other's lock — meaning mutual exclusion is silently broken for the entire mixed-deployment window.
findings.location: Design section: 'Expand-contract cutover' paragraph; AC4 (`lock_backend = file | ref` switch)
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR cutover section, document: (1) `lock_backend` is a cluster-wide setting — all clones must be updated in the same config commit; (2) add a startup check in the `file` backend path that warns if `refs/reconciler/lock` already exists on origin (indicating another clone is using `ref` mode); (3) make the migration a single PR that updates the config for all deployment targets together.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a fleet-synchronization procedure for the lock_backend flip: document how all coordinating clones (local + GA bridge) must switch lock_backend within the same deploy step (or define a brief dual-check shim where the 'ref' backend also reads the 'file' lock during transition) so the overlap window is bounded and known.
findings.finding: During the lock_backend=file→ref expand-contract window, clones running the file backend and clones running the ref backend are mutually invisible as lock holders: a 'file' clone sees .reconciler-pass-lock on the tickets branch, while a 'ref' clone sees refs/reconciler/lock, and neither check the other's store. The plan describes both backends as simultaneously active but provides no mechanism to ensure all coordinating clones (local + GitHub-Actions bridge) flip their lock_backend atomically — leaving an overlap window where concurrent passes can both acquire their respective locks and run simultaneously.
findings.location: Design section — 'Expand-contract cutover' paragraph; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lock_lease_secs` registration to C3's `_config_schema.py` scope (alongside `lock_backend`), include it in C3's AC, and confirm C4 inherits or supersedes it.
findings.finding: C2 (7522) explicitly delegates registration of the `lock_lease_secs` config key in `_config_schema.py` to C3, but C3 (2711) only mentions adding `[reconciler] lock_backend`; `lock_lease_secs` is absent from C3's What section and all six of C3's ACs.
findings.location: child 7522-129b-1380-418c (delegation) and child 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 (gap)
findings.suggested_fix: In C3 (2711), explicitly add `lock_lease_secs` to the `[reconciler]` config schema alongside `lock_backend` (positive int, default 120), document it in `docs/config.md`, and add an AC line: '`lock_lease_secs` is registered in `_config_schema.py` with a default of 120 s and governs both the lease duration and the heartbeat interval (`max(1, lease//3)`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Design-body paragraph specifying the already-initialized-tracker migration mechanism (e.g., a `rebar migrate` sub-command, an auto-patch on `rebar sync`, or an explicit repair step) so AC5's 'migration' assertion has a described implementation to verify against.
findings.finding: AC5 mentions a migration that 'also updates already-initialized trackers' as a deliverable, but the Design body describes only the `.gitattributes` change and new ref backend — it does not describe the mechanism for migrating already-initialized trackers (e.g., a git command, a `rebar init --repair` flag, or an auto-migrate on first run). This is an orphan AC sub-deliverable with no Design coverage.
findings.location: AC5 ('via a migration that also updates already-initialized trackers') vs Design section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback plan to explicitly include reverting the .gitattributes commit on the tickets orphan branch (re-adding '.reconciler-* merge=ours') as a prerequisite step before lock_backend can be safely reverted to 'file', or document that C4 (remove file backend) is a one-way door once the .gitattributes migration is pushed.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback path ('revert lock_backend to file') is incomplete because it does not account for the .gitattributes removal on the tickets orphan branch. Once AC5 lands — removing '.reconciler-* merge=ours' from init.py:57 and committing the updated .gitattributes to the tickets branch on origin — rolling lock_backend back to 'file' reintroduces .reconciler-* lock files on the tickets branch without the merge=ours safety net. The two rollback actions (.gitattributes revert on the shared branch + lock_backend config revert) are non-atomic and cannot be coordinated across already-distributed clones.
findings.location: AC5 ('.reconciler-* removed from the merge=ours .gitattributes … via a migration that also updates already-initialized trackers'); Design 'Expand-contract cutover' rollback path
findings.suggested_fix: Mark C4 as a one-way door: once the .gitattributes entry is removed and pushed to origin, note in the ADR that rolling back to lock_backend=file requires a manual git commit on the tickets branch to restore the merge=ours entry before any clone can safely acquire a file-backend lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in C3 that `acquire_pass_lock` catches `RefLockHeldError` from `_ref_lock.acquire()` and re-raises it as `ReconcileLockError` (or make `RefLockHeldError` a subclass), and add a unit test covering this translation.
findings.finding: C3 (2711) specifies that `acquire_pass_lock` must raise `ReconcileLockError` on a rejected CAS (to match existing file-lock semantics), but does not specify the translation layer from C1's `RefLockHeldError` (raised by `_ref_lock.acquire()`) to `ReconcileLockError` (the exception `__main__.py` catches). If the translation shim is omitted, `RefLockHeldError` propagates uncaught through `_advisory_lock.py` into `__main__.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_advisory_lock.py`'s `acquire_pass_lock`, under the `lock_backend=ref` branch, wrap the `_ref_lock.acquire()` call with `except RefLockHeldError as e: raise ReconcileLockError(...) from e`. Alternatively, define `RefLockHeldError` as a subclass of `ReconcileLockError`. Document the exception hierarchy in C3's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to C3 (2711) requiring the ADR to be extended with the `lock_backend` expand-contract rationale, the rollback procedure (`lock_backend=file`), and the cutover checklist.
findings.finding: C3 (2711) introduces the `lock_backend` switch and the rollback path — the substance of parent AC-D ('the ref-lock design + rollback path are recorded in a new ADR') — but C3 contains no instruction to extend or update the ADR, leaving the rollback path potentially unrecorded.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C3's AC: '- [ ] The `docs/adr/` ref-lock entry is extended to record the `lock_backend = file | ref` expand-contract pattern, the default-flip timeline, and the operator rollback procedure (`lock_backend = file` in `[reconciler]`).' Assign this extension explicitly to C3's implementation scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit, observable 'proven' gate for C4 in the ADR (e.g., 'ref backend runs ≥ N reconcile passes across both local and CI clones with zero `LeaseLostError` incidents over a 2-week soak period, confirmed by a tracking issue before merging the C4 cleanup PR').
findings.finding: The plan states the file backend and `merge=ours`/b859 band-aid are removed 'only after the ref backend is proven (C4),' but never defines what 'proven' means — no duration, no metric, no observable gate. Without a defined criterion, C4 could fire prematurely (removing the rollback path before the ref backend has accumulated enough production evidence), or never fire (leaving dead code indefinitely). Once C4 fires and the file backend is deleted, rollback requires a non-trivial code change and redeploy, not the 'cheap' revert the T12 criterion requires.
findings.location: Design section: 'Expand-contract cutover' paragraph; AC4 and AC5/AC6 (removal of merge=ours and b859 band-aid)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Design the tiny-commit fallback path at least at blob-schema + CAS-semantics level before C1 AC0 runs, so a spike failure results in a known implementation swap rather than a redesign; include how heartbeat renew works (new commit on each renew? amendment?) and how stale commit chains are pruned.
findings.finding: GitHub may reject a `git push` where the ref resolves to a blob OID rather than a commit OID. Standard GitHub branch protection, object validation, and the receive-pack hook typically expect refs under `refs/heads/*` to point at commits and may reject arbitrary `refs/*` pointing at blob objects. The plan names this as 'load-bearing and unproven' (C1 AC0) and scopes a spike, but the contingency path ('ref → tiny-commit instead of ref → blob') is only named — not designed. If the blob path fails, the team has no specified fallback schema, CAS semantics for commit-backed refs, or heartbeat renew protocol, meaning C1 AC0 failure could require re-designing AC1, AC2, and AC3 before C2 begins.
findings.location: Design section, 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven'; AC line AC1 ('bare ref -> blob')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC1) explicitly requiring that `_is_cas_mismatch` (or its successor) is parameterized on ref name and verified by a unit test that it correctly identifies CAS mismatches on `refs/reconciler/*` refs.
findings.finding: The Design body describes `_is_cas_mismatch` as needing to be 'generalized to accept the ref name' (currently hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets`), which is a code deliverable, but no acceptance criterion verifies this generalization.
findings.location: Design section ('_is_cas_mismatch (`:415`, line 425) hard-codes refs/heads/tickets and must be generalized') vs AC list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the access-control contract for `refs/reconciler/*` on GitHub (e.g., whether a repository ruleset restricts writes to this ref namespace to only the bridge bot / GITHUB_TOKEN identity), or document why the existing `contents: write` trust boundary is acceptable for a single-tenant deployment.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new git ref namespace (`refs/reconciler/lock`, `refs/reconciler/gate`) writable by anyone with `contents: write` on the GitHub repository, but specifies no access-control contract (branch-protection ruleset or push restriction) for this namespace. A collaborator or compromised `GITHUB_TOKEN` could forge a lock blob with an arbitrarily large `lease_secs` or `fence` to permanently wedge pass coordination — the exact failure mode the self-healing design targets.
findings.location: Design section — 'Move pass-lock and phase-gate OFF the working tree into bare git refs `refs/reconciler/lock` and `refs/reconciler/gate`' / AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a GitHub repository ruleset (via `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`) that restricts pushes to `refs/reconciler/**` to the bridge-bot actor (the Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` identity for the `reconcile-bridge.yml` workflow). Document this in the ADR alongside the rollback path. If a ruleset is impractical, document explicitly in the ADR that the threat model accepts internal-collaborator write access to the lock namespace and explain why the idempotent-pass guarantee bounds the blast radius.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the migration behavior for the retired `lock_max_retries` config key (e.g., emit a deprecation warning and continue, or raise a config-parse error with a message pointing to `lock_lease_secs`) so the executor does not have to choose arbitrarily.
findings.finding: The plan removes the `lock_max_retries` config key and its env aliases (AC6) but does not specify the migration behavior for existing tracker configs that still contain `lock_max_retries` — whether the parser should warn, hard-error, or silently ignore the retired key is unspecified.
findings.location: AC6: '`lock_max_retries` (`_config_schema.py:334,445`) + env aliases + their tests are removed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6: 'Parsing a config that still contains `lock_max_retries` emits a `DeprecationWarning` naming `lock_lease_secs` as the replacement and proceeds (does not hard-error).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the migration policy for `lock_max_retries` removal: either (a) add a deprecation-warning phase where the key is accepted but ignored when `lock_backend = ref`, then removed in a subsequent release, or (b) explicitly document that it raises a `ConfigError` on upgrade and add a migration note to the ADR and release notes.
findings.finding: The `lock_max_retries` config key (and its env aliases) is removed without specifying a migration/deprecation policy for existing deployments that have it set — the plan doesn't state whether the ref backend silently ignores it, emits a deprecation warning, or raises a validation error.
findings.location: AC item 6 / `_config_schema.py:334,445`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a migration step: when `lock_backend = ref` (or `lock_backend` is absent and the ref backend is the new default), if `lock_max_retries` is still present in config, emit a `DeprecationWarning` log line and ignore it rather than raising a validation error. Remove the key from `_config_schema.py` only after one release cycle.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit doc-cleanup item to the ADR or AC: audit and update all docstrings, comments, and runbook references in `_advisory_lock.py` and related files that mention `.reconciler-pass-lock` / `.reconciler-phase-gate` file paths, replacing them with the new `refs/reconciler/lock` / `refs/reconciler/gate` ref locations and the CAS-break recovery procedure.
findings.finding: Existing documentation, docstrings, and references to the `.reconciler-pass-lock` and `.reconciler-phase-gate` file paths will become stale after the migration, but no plan item explicitly covers updating or removing them.
findings.location: Design section / `_advisory_lock.py` / out-of-scope callouts

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief measurement/derivation note for the 120 s lease default — either cite observed p95 pass durations from CI logs or add a TODO to emit pass-duration telemetry in C1 so the default can be validated and tightened before C4 (removal of the file backend).
findings.finding: The 120 s lease default and the lease/3 heartbeat cadence are asserted without any measurement plan or derivation from observed pass-duration data, leaving the defaults un-validated.
findings.location: Design section — 'default lease 120 s, heartbeat every ~lease/3, both configurable via a new [reconciler] config key lock_lease_secs'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR and/or the `[reconciler]` config-key documentation, record the reasoning: 'observed pass p95 duration = X s → lease chosen as 2× p95; heartbeat = lease/3 to tolerate 2 consecutive push failures.' If the data does not yet exist, mark the default as provisional and add a follow-up task to tune it once the ref backend has run in production for one sprint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate GitHub Actions `concurrency:` group (with `cancel-in-progress: false`) as prior art for preventing overlapping CI reconciler passes before committing to the full ref-CAS + heartbeat-daemon architecture; document why it is insufficient if rejected.
findings.finding: GitHub Actions' native `concurrency:` group (with `cancel-in-progress: false`) is unexamined prior art that directly prevents overlapping reconciler passes on the CI runner — the primary contention surface named in the plan — without any custom ref machinery, lease logic, or daemon threads.
findings.location: Design section / Context: 'feat/reconcile-continuous-loop … explicitly relies on concurrency + the reconciler pass-lock to prevent overlapping passes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short Design section paragraph: 'We evaluated GitHub Actions `concurrency:` group — it prevents overlapping *CI* runs but does not coordinate local-clone passes against CI runs or local-clone vs local-clone; the ref-lock is therefore still required for the cross-clone case. GitHub native concurrency can be added as a defence-in-depth guard on the workflow but does not replace the ref-lock.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
